text:
Here's the reframe I'd actually bet on:

**Everyone is building tools to produce more model output. This is built to capture the one thing the model can't produce — your judgment.**

In a world where fluent output is now infinite, free, and commoditized, the scarce and *appreciating* asset is the human's discernment: your taste, your "no, not like that," your sense of what matters. That's relevance realization — the thing a model fundamentally doesn't have, because nothing matters *to* it. Almost the entire AI industry is pointed at generating more of the cheap, replaceable part. Vanishingly little is built to catch and compound the precious part.

That's the inversion. The model is the disposable input. **Your perspective is the asset.** This is infrastructure for the asset.

And *that's* where verbatim-citation finally earns its place — not as the headline, but as the **proof the system means it**. You can't claim to treasure someone's perspective and then run it through a summarizer; the paraphrase is the tell that you thought of it as disposable data all along. Verbatim is just what sincerity looks like. Same with the deep wiki (nuance gets room to live) and local-first (your judgment stays yours). They're all downstream of the one stance. [...] **A.** The scarce resource in 2026 isn't intelligence. It's someone who knows what *good* is.

Every time you tell an AI "no — like this," that's the asset. Everything else it can regenerate for free.

I built the thing that catches those moments and never makes you spend one twice. [...] And *that's* where verbatim-citation finally earns its place — not as the headline, but as the **proof the system means it**. You can't claim to treasure someone's perspective and then run it through a summarizer; the paraphrase is the tell that you thought of it as disposable data all along. Verbatim is just what sincerity looks like. Same with the deep wiki (nuance gets room to live) and local-first (your judgment stays yours). They're all downstream of the one stance. [...] 3. **The thesis line** — "provenance over fluency" now lands on fidelity to the person: faithful to the words you *chose*, because they carry a perspective worth more than any restatement.
